insights: Cost Reduction goals across all departments have dramatically extended durations (mean 256–285 days), more than 2.1× longer than all other goal categories (mean 76–100 days), confirming this is a category-level systemic issue—not a department-specific problem.

Finance appears to be the primary concern only because it owns 62.8% (108 of 172) of all Cost Reduction goals, creating a volume-driven illusion of departmental underperformance despite having the second-shortest mean duration (258.92 days) within that category.

Marketing’s Cost Reduction goals exhibit the longest mean duration (284.64 days), and all departments show uniformly extended durations across states, priorities, target percentages, completion levels, and metric types—indicating no operational or structural variable tested explains the category-wide delay.

Within Cost Reduction, Finance demonstrates lower owner concentration (HHI 0.0189 vs 0.0259) and higher owner diversity (67 vs 45 unique owners), ruling out ownership bottlenecks or over-reliance on few individuals as a driver of extended durations.

The extended duration of Cost Reduction goals persists regardless of goal state—Cancelled Cost Reduction goals average 289 days (vs 175 days overall), and even Planned ones average 240 days (vs 88–103 days for other categories)—suggesting inherent process friction in initiation, execution, or closure logic specific to cost reduction work.

No evidence supports department-specific root causes: Finance performs slightly better than average within Cost Reduction, and all cross-category drivers (priority, metric, target, completion rate) show consistent non-discrimination—implicating standardized goal design, approval workflows, or success criteria as likely systemic levers.
summary: The investigation conclusively shows that extended goal durations are not a Finance department problem but a systemic characteristic of the 'Cost Reduction' category across all departments—driven neither by departmental execution, ownership patterns, goal state, priority, nor metric type. Finance accounts for the majority of these goals (62.8%), creating a false impression of departmental inefficiency, while Marketing actually exhibits the longest durations. The uniformity of delays across all subgroups—including Planned and Cancelled goals—points to foundational issues in how Cost Reduction goals are defined, scoped, resourced, or measured organization-wide. Recommended next focus: audit the end-to-end goal lifecycle for Cost Reduction objectives, specifically examining scoping rigor, milestone definition, cross-functional dependency management, and success validation protocols.
